update: Baby robins poop in the nest, but they do not make a mess. Almost immediately after eating, the baby wiggles its rear end up. The poop is wrapped in a strong, thick mucus membrane called a fecal sac. This sac acts like a disposable diaper. Parent robins take care of the poop in two ways: (1) When the chicks are very young, the parents eat the sacs. The babies cannot digest all their food. The poop contains nutrients that give the parents energy to keep hunting. or (2) As the babies grow, the parents stop eating the sacs. Instead, they grab the sac in their beak, fly a safe distance away from the nest, and drop it. This keeps the nest clean, hides the smell from predators, and keeps the babies healthy. Check out the video in the gallery.
funfact: What they look like: The American Robin is about 10 inches long (25 cm), and has a brick-red breast, with gray wings, head and back. The short beak is yellow. It has a white throat with black stripes; the males throat is darker than the female. The lower belly is primarily white. It has a long gray/black tail. Males and females look similar, but the males have a darker head (often black) and the female has paler color. There is a broken white eye-ring that surrounds their dark eyes. The juvenile's under parts are tinged with cinnamon and heavily spotted with brown.
